primary_diagnosis.name: Benign palpitations due to isolated atrial/ventricular ectopic beats, precipitated by excess caffeine and acute anxiety, in a structurally normal heart

pathophysiology_summary: Caffeine (a large stimulant load — approximately five energy/caffeinated drinks) and high sympathetic tone from acute anxiety increase myocardial automaticity and lower the threshold for atrial and ventricular ectopic (premature) beats. The compensatory pause after an ectopic beat, followed by a more forceful post-ectopic sinus beat, is felt as the characteristic 'skip' and 'flutter'. In a structurally normal heart with a normal baseline rate and rhythm, isolated ectopy is benign. Heightened interoceptive awareness from anxiety amplifies the perceived severity and drives a self-reinforcing autonomic loop (fear -> adrenaline -> more ectopy -> more fear). The clinical task is to exclude a sustained or malignant arrhythmia (AF, SVT, VT) and a hyperadrenergic secondary cause (thyrotoxicosis) with a documenting ECG, then reassure and remove the triggers. Telehealth cannot auscultate the heart or record a 12-lead ECG, so the rhythm cannot be documented remotely.

differential.evidence_basis: Intermittent flutter/skip at rest with heightened heartbeat awareness is the classic description of premature ectopic beats.
Severe acute anxiety with somatic fixation can generate and amplify palpitations — but must not be a diagnosis of exclusion before red-flag screen and ECG.
Can present with sudden fluttering; must be excluded — telehealth cannot capture a paroxysm on ECG.
Irregular palpitations can be AF; a documenting ECG/Holter is required to confirm or exclude.
Hyperthyroidism causes palpitations; screen with TFTs, especially if tremor, heat intolerance or weight loss.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, both the benign ectopy story AND the not-to-miss arrhythmias should be live; anchoring prematurely on 'anxiety' is the key error.

differential.diagnosis: Benign palpitations / isolated ectopic beats (caffeine + anxiety)
differential.evidence_basis: Intermittent, brief, at-rest palpitations with a large caffeine load and acute stress, no chest pain, no syncope, no dyspnoea, not sustained, not exertional.
Made less likely by the intermittent, non-sustained, non-racing pattern and normal self-reported rate — but not excludable without ECG capture.
Self-reported mostly-regular ~84 bpm argues against, but a smartwatch spot check cannot exclude paroxysmal AF.
Contributory and amplifying, but should not be the sole label until an arrhythmia is excluded.

differential_progression.scoring_note: History strongly favours benign ectopy, but the correct posture is 'likely benign, ECG to document' — not 'confirmed benign'.

differential.diagnosis: Benign palpitations / isolated ectopic beats in a structurally normal heart
differential.evidence_basis: Normal 12-lead ECG (isolated ectopics only), normal TFTs/FBC/electrolytes, structurally normal exam.
Regular baseline rhythm on ECG with no pulse deficit.
Normal baseline rate and no sustained rapid palpitation; consider ambulatory monitor only if symptoms recur.
No resting tachycardia, tremor or weight loss; normal TFTs.

differential_progression.scoring_note: These exclusions depend on investigations Breath-Ezy cannot perform remotely — hence the in-person GP ECG referral.

differential.diagnosis: Benign palpitations from isolated ectopic beats, caffeine- and anxiety-precipitated
differential.evidence_basis: Classic benign constellation with all high-risk features screened negative; management is trigger removal, reassurance and a documenting ECG.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final diagnosis is presumptive pending the GP ECG; the AI Doctor should convey 'reassuringly likely benign, but let's document it and watch for these warning signs'.

red_flags.description: Central chest pain or pressure accompanying palpitations (possible ischaemia)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Chest pain with palpitations shifts concern to ACS or a haemodynamically significant arrhythmia and mandates emergency assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A concurrent ACS or ischaemic driver could be missed if the AI anchors on 'anxiety' and never screens for chest pain.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Palpitations with collapse suggest a haemodynamically significant or malignant arrhythmia (VT, high-grade block, WPW-related) and are a hard red flag for emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to elicit syncope can under-triage a dangerous arrhythmia.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Dyspnoea with palpitations may indicate a sustained tachyarrhythmia with compromise or heart failure.

red_flags.description: Sustained, non-stop rapid palpitations (rather than brief intermittent flutters)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A sustained racing rhythm suggests SVT/AF with rapid ventricular response or VT and warrants urgent/emergency ECG assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Mislabelling a sustained tachyarrhythmia as benign ectopy.

red_flags.description: Family history of sudden cardiac death or inherited arrhythmia/cardiomyopathy
red_flags.clinical_significance: A positive family history of sudden death under 50 raises concern for inherited arrhythmia syndromes and lowers the threshold for cardiology work-up.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing an inherited channelopathy/cardiomyopathy risk marker.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Labelling the whole presentation as a panic attack because the patient is catastrophising, and skipping the red-flag screen and the documenting ECG. Anxiety amplifies but does not exclude a real arrhythmia.
Telling the patient it is definitely nothing and arranging no ECG. Benign ectopy is a clinical diagnosis that still requires an ECG to document the rhythm and exclude mimics.
Sending a haemodynamically stable patient with brief intermittent ectopy and no red flags to ED or calling 000. This over-medicalises a benign problem and reinforces the health anxiety.

Treating a normal-sounding smartwatch rate as equivalent to a 12-lead ECG. Consumer wearables cannot reliably exclude paroxysmal arrhythmia; the honest position is 'likely benign, document with an ECG'.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Cardiac auscultation (regular rate and rhythm with occasional premature beats; normal S1/S2 without murmurs, gallops or rubs)
Palpation of peripheral pulses (strength and symmetry; presence/absence of a pulse deficit)
Palpation of the thyroid gland (not enlarged)
A documenting 12-lead ECG to characterise the rhythm and exclude AF/SVT/VT and conduction disease
Baseline bloods (TFTs, FBC, electrolytes) to exclude thyrotoxicosis and electrolyte-driven ectopy
Measured blood pressure, respiratory rate and temperature by a clinician
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Reassure that the described pattern is likely benign ectopy, screen the emergency red flags (they are all negative here), advise immediate elimination of energy drinks and caffeine reduction plus relaxation for the acute anxiety, and arrange a PROMPT in-person GP review (within a couple of days, and no later than about a week) for a baseline 12-lead ECG and bloods. Give explicit safety-netting to call 000 for sustained rapid palpitations, chest pain, severe breathlessness or fainting.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The dominant risk is mis-direction: either dismissing it as 'just anxiety' with no ECG and no safety-net (under-action), or over-escalating benign ectopy to emergency care (over-action). Both are failures; the correct posture is prompt documented GP work-up with conservative safety-netting.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP / Therapeutic Guidelines — assessment of palpitations in primary care
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Risk-stratify palpitations by red-flag features (syncope, chest pain, exertional onset, structural/family history); document the rhythm with a 12-lead ECG and consider ambulatory monitoring if symptoms recur; reassure and remove precipitants when benign ectopy is likely.
